topicTitle: Topic 2: Comparing Linear and Exponential Growth

value.text: Linear and exponential functions both model change over time, but in very different ways:

conceptSummary.value: \text{Linear: } y = mx + b \qquad \text{Exponential: } y = a b^x

value.text: Early on, a linear function might appear to grow faster, but eventually an exponential function surpasses it because it compounds multiplicatively.
when a fixed amount is added or subtracted (e.g., salary increases by $1000 each year).
when change is proportional to the current value (e.g., population grows 5% per year).
Exponential graphs curve upward (growth) or downward (decay).
Identify whether change is additive (linear) or multiplicative (exponential).
Write equations for both models given a situation or data.
Compare values over time to see when exponential overtakes linear.
Recognize patterns in tables and graphs that distinguish the two.
A quantity increases by 10 each year.

Constant difference → linear.

Another quantity increases by 10% each year.
First is linear, second is exponential.
At first, linear grows faster, but around
A worker earns $30,000 and gets a $2,000 raise yearly (linear):
A startup grows revenue by 10% annually (exponential):
The exponential model surpasses the linear one.
Exponential wins in the long term.

solution.value: \boxed{\text{Linear is faster first, exponential is faster later}}

value.text: Write one example of a linear model and one of an exponential model that both start at 200.

A savings account grows by $200 per year. Another account grows by 6% per year from $1000.  
  Write both models and describe which will be greater after 10 years.
Savings account 1: grows by $200/year from $1000. Linear.
Savings account 2: grows 6% per year from $1000. Exponential.

A car’s value decreases linearly by $1500 per year starting at $20,000, while another model decreases by 10% each year.  
  Write both equations and describe which loses value faster over time.
Car model 1: loses $1500 per year, starting $20{,}000. Linear.
Car model 2: loses 10% per year. Keeps 90%.
Early: 10% of 20{,}000 is 2000, which is more than 1500. So exponential decay drops faster at first.
Later: As value shrinks, 10% of the remaining amount becomes smaller than 1500, so linear eventually loses value faster.

solution.value: \boxed{\text{Exponential loses faster first, linear loses faster later}}

Which graph shows a constant rate of change: the line or the curve?
Constant rate of change means constant slope. That is the line.

solution.value: \boxed{\text{The line has constant rate of change}}

value.text: Which graph represents a constant percent change?
Constant percent change means multiply by same factor repeatedly. That is the exponential curve.

Describe how the slopes or ratios appear different in graphs of linear vs exponential functions.

In a linear graph, slope is constant and the line is straight.  
  In an exponential graph, the slope keeps increasing (for growth), so the curve gets steeper.

solution.value: \boxed{\text{Linear: constant slope. Exponential: slope keeps changing}}

SAT clue for exponential:

per year, doubles every 6 days, loses 12% each cycle, or "multiplied by the same factor each period" indicate exponential.  
  Words like "increases by 400 each month" indicate linear.
